task: Production-ready upgrade for binayah-ultimate gamification dashboard: (1) AUTH — sign up/login with email+password and Google OAuth, gate dashboard behind auth, tie currentUser to authenticated account via MongoDB; (2) CACHE BUG — detect and gracefully recover from stale localStorage after rebuild (blank screen fix); (3) RESPONSIVE — make list/kanban/overview views work on mobile and tablet, full-screen drawers on mobile; (4) PERFORMANCE — lazy-load Chat/ActivityFeed/Kanban/Overview panels, code-split Stage.tsx mockup imports; (5) LOCKED CARDS — block ALL edit interactions on locked pipelines (subtasks, comments, status, stages, name/desc), make lock state visually prominent; (6) EMAIL NOTIFICATIONS — send email when stage is claimed or goes active.

stages.title: Auth foundation — next-auth, login UI, whitelist, Prajeesh
stages.spec:
## Why this will be good
The login screen is the first and only thing every team member sees — it must feel like the command center, not a generic form. Dense, dark, intentional. On open: the brand mark, one quiet line of copy, Google primary, email/password secondary, denied-email state that feels designed (not a red alert box). Benchmark: Linear's sign-in, Superhuman's invite screen, Vercel's dashboard login. Production-ready: loading state on both auth methods, disabled state while in flight, denied-email messaging that names the user's email back to them (so they know they typed it right), keyboard-first (Enter submits, Tab order makes sense), and 390px mobile layout that does not just shrink-fit a desktop form.

## Goal
Replace the "who are you" onboarding step with real authentication. Every route except /login and /api/auth/* requires a valid session. Session carries a stable fixedUserId that the dashboard uses as currentUser.

## Scope — files to touch
- NEW: src/app/login/page.tsx — the login screen
- NEW: src/app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts — next-auth v4 route handler (App Router compatible)
- NEW: src/lib/auth.ts — NextAuthOptions, ADMIN_EMAIL_MAP, helpers
- NEW: src/lib/AuthUser.ts — Mongoose model (email, passwordHash, fixedUserId, createdAt)
- NEW: src/app/api/auth/signup/route.ts — credentials signup with bcrypt (whitelist-enforced)
- NEW: middleware.ts at project root — redirect unauth'd to /login
- NEW: src/types/next-auth.d.ts — extend Session with fixedUserId
- MODIFY: src/lib/data.ts — add prajeesh to USERS_DEFAULT (role: "The PM", distinct color from the existing 5 — pick from Tailwind's orange/rose/teal range that is not already used)
- MODIFY: src/components/Dashboard.tsx — read session via useSession(), derive currentUser from session.fixedUserId, skip onboarding identity step when session present
- MODIFY: src/components/Onboarding.tsx — hide identity step (step 1) when session.fixedUserId is known; keep avatar + theme steps
- MODIFY: src/app/layout.tsx — wrap in SessionProvider
- MODIFY: package.json — add next-auth@^4, bcryptjs, @types/bcryptjs to dependencies (NOT devDependencies)

## Concrete behavior
- **Providers:** Google OAuth (client id/secret from .env.shared) + Credentials (email + bcrypt password against AuthUser collection).
- **Whitelist:** On every sign-in attempt (both providers), check email against ADMIN_EMAIL_MAP. If not present → deny with a specific error ("This email isn't on the access list. Contact Prajeesh to be added."). Never silently succeed with no user.
- **ADMIN_EMAIL_MAP** (hardcoded in src/lib/auth.ts — do NOT rely solely on comma-split env var; the plannerAnswers explicitly include multi-email users):
  ```
  anna@prosper-fi.com → anna
  dev@prosper-fi.com → anna
  anna@gmail.com → anna
  aakarshit@prosper-fi.com → aakarshit
  uk@prosper-fi.com → usama
  mamr@binayah.com → ahsan
  pm@binayah.com → prajeesh
  ```
  abdallah has no confirmed email yet — keep the fixedUserId in USERS_DEFAULT but they can't log in until an email is added. Leave a clear TODO comment.
- **Session shape:** JWT strategy. token/session both carry { email, fixedUserId, name, image }. No DB adapter.
- **Signup flow:** /login has a "first time? set a password" toggle. POST /api/auth/signup validates email is in whitelist, hashes password with bcrypt (12 rounds), upserts into AuthUser. Then user can sign in with credentials. Google OAuth does not require pre-signup.
- **Middleware:** Redirects any unauthenticated request to /login?callbackUrl=<original>. Allows /login, /api/auth/*, /_next/*, static assets.
- **Onboarding change:** when session has fixedUserId, set currentUser to that id on mount and force onboardingStep past the identity step. Avatar + theme customisation stay.
- **Logout:** visible sign-out in the existing user menu/header area. Uses signOut({ callbackUrl: '/login' }).
- **NEXTAUTH_URL:** document in plan summary that Vercel env var for THIS project must be set to https://dashboard-gamification.vercel.app (not the properties URL in .env.shared). DEV sets this via Vercel API.

## Login screen design intent (designer briefs this)
This is the command center's front door. It must look like a piece of the product, not a layer on top. Full-viewport dark background using t.bg, centered composition on desktop, top-aligned on mobile. One brand lockup (logo/wordmark), one line of copy (suggestion: "Binayah Ultimate" + muted "Project command center" — designer refines). Google button is primary (full-width, prominent), email+password is secondary and collapses behind a "sign in with email" toggle or sits below a hairline divider. Deny state renders inline below the form, not a modal or toast. Loading state locks buttons and shows a subtle inline spinner that uses t.text tokens. Must support all 4 themes — on /login specifically, default to warroom unless user had one saved. No stock illustrations, no marketing fluff.

## States that must be covered
- Loading (first paint — SSR or skeleton)
- In-flight sign-in (buttons disabled, inline spinner)
- Error: invalid credentials ("Wrong email or password")
- Error: not on whitelist (quote the email back)
- Error: network/unknown ("Something went wrong. Try again.")
- Already signed in (redirect to / immediately)
- Mobile 390px — the form doesn't feel cramped; Google button still tap-friendly (≥44px)

## Acceptance criteria
- Fresh browser → hitting / redirects to /login.
- Signing in with pm@binayah.com via Google → dashboard loads with currentUser = prajeesh, who appears in users panel with the PM role.
- Signing in with dev@prosper-fi.com via Google → currentUser = anna.
- Non-whitelisted email (e.g. random@gmail.com) → denied with named email in error.
- Refresh after sign-in → stays signed in (JWT cookie).
- Sign out → /login.
- Onboarding no longer shows the identity step post-login; avatar + theme steps still work.
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
- All login UI uses t.* tokens; no hardcoded hex.

## DO NOT
- Do NOT install next-auth v5/Auth.js (beta). Use v4.
- Do NOT silently allow a non-whitelisted email through.
- Do NOT write CSS custom properties for theme colors.
- Do NOT touch the PipelineState schema (AuthUser is a separate collection).
- Do NOT prescribe exact Tailwind class strings in DEV's code — designer & DEV choose spacing, corner radii, animations.
stages.demoPath: 1. In incognito, visit https://dashboard-gamification.vercel.app → redirects to /login. 2. Click 'Continue with Google' → Google consent → back to / as signed-in user (currentUser reflects mapped fixedUserId). 3. Sign out → back at /login. 4. Try email 'random@gmail.com' + any password → see denied state naming the email. 5. Sign up with pm@binayah.com + a password → sign in with credentials → prajeesh is currentUser and appears in users panel. 6. Refresh → still signed in. 7. Resize to 390px → login feels native, not cramped.

stages.title: Cache-version recovery + production hardening across all functions
stages.spec:
## Why this will be good
The "blank screen after deploy" bug is embarrassing — and it's one symptom of a broader pattern where failures are silent. This stage kills that class of bug and makes every existing feature (messages, comments, subtasks, reactions, avatar gen, AI chat, PDF export) fail loudly and recover gracefully. Benchmark: Linear's optimistic updates that rollback visibly on error, Superhuman's inline error toasts. Production-ready: every fetch has a catch, every user action has a visible success/failure affordance, every long list has a bound.

## Goal
(a) Ship a SCHEMA_VERSION-based localStorage recovery so a rebuild never leaves users on a blank screen. (b) Harden every existing feature per the production brief — messages, comments, subtasks, reactions, PDF, avatar gen, AI chat — with consistent error UI, optimistic+rollback, length/empty guards, and bounded pagination.

## Scope — files to touch
- FIX: src/components/LoginClient.tsx — (a) error messages currently render with double "//" prefix (e.g. "// Invalid email") — strip the leading "//"; (b) the "Not whitelisted" message for signup vs login shows identical copy — differentiate them (e.g. login: "Access denied — contact your admin", signup: "This email is not on the approved list").
- NEW: src/lib/version.ts — `export const SCHEMA_VERSION = "v2";`
- NEW: src/components/ui/Toast.tsx — small themed toast primitive (if none exists)
- MODIFY: src/components/Dashboard.tsx — version check on mount (BEFORE any state reads), clear all lsSet() keys, show 2s toast, reload
- MODIFY: src/components/ChatPanel.tsx — disable send when empty, optimistic message + rollback on 4xx/5xx, inline error badge, XSS-safe rendering (text only)
- MODIFY: src/components/Stage.tsx — subtask add/edit/check: optimistic + rollback, empty guard, max length; comments: same; reactions: toggle-on-double-click correctness
- MODIFY: src/components/ActivityFeed.tsx — bound to last N (e.g. 200) client-side, human-relative timestamps ("2m ago"), no crash on empty
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/activity/route.ts — server-side cap inserts (keep last N in array, trim on PATCH)
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/messages/route.ts — verify rateLimit applied, checkContentLength applied, typed JSON error responses
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/comments/route.ts — typed error responses, validateStageKey on every code path
- MODIFY: src/app/api/generate-pfp/route.ts — typed error response, client handles and shows toast
- MODIFY: src/app/api/chat/route.ts — verify 429 surfaces client-side with a visible message (not console-only)
- MODIFY: src/lib/generatePDF.ts (or the caller component) — wrap in try/catch, show toast on failure, handle empty pipelines and unicode
- MODIFY: src/lib/apiSync.ts — standardise error shape returned to callers so components can show consistent error UI

## Concrete behavior
- **Version recovery:** On Dashboard mount (first effect, before state reads): read lsGet("schemaVersion"). If it !== SCHEMA_VERSION: set a `recovering` flag, render a centred 2-second themed toast ("Dashboard updated — refreshing…"), enumerate every key written by lsSet() across the codebase (DEV audits this — grep for lsSet() calls), remove them, set the new version, then window.location.reload(). NOTE: do NOT clear next-auth session cookies or the auth token — only clear lsSet() localStorage keys. The session is independent of the schema version.. After reload: fresh state loads from Mongo, version is now current, no loop.
- **Toast primitive:** dark-theme, t.* tokens only, top-or-bottom-center, 3–4s default auto-dismiss, manual-dismiss optional, stacks ≤3. No external library. Used for: recovery toast, action errors (message failed, PDF failed, avatar failed), AI 429, comment rejected.
- **Optimistic+rollback pattern:** for message/comment/subtask/reaction: render immediately with a `pending: true` flag, call the API, on success clear pending, on failure remove the optimistic item AND show toast ("Couldn't send — tap to retry").
- **Empty-input guards:** disable buttons when input is whitespace-only. No empty rows ever saved.
- **Length limits:** message ≤2000 chars, comment ≤1000, subtask ≤200 — enforced client-side (input maxLength + counter) AND server-side (checkContentLength). Server is source of truth.
- **Activity cap:** server trims on write to last 200 items. Client paginates/virtualises if needed (or just renders cap).
- **Typed errors:** every API route returns `{ error: string, code?: string }` on failure, with appropriate status. No raw 500 text.
- **AI 429 handling:** client catches 429 and shows "AI is rate-limited — try again in a minute" toast.
- **No console.error without user-visible effect** for any action the user initiated.

## States that must be covered (everywhere)
- Empty: empty message history, empty activity feed, no subtasks, no comments — all have warm empty states, not blank panes.
- Loading: fetchState, PDF generation, avatar generation — explicit spinners/skeletons using t.*.
- Error: every fetch call has a visible failure UX (toast or inline).
- Recovery: the schema-version bump path verified in staging.

## Acceptance criteria
- Change SCHEMA_VERSION locally, reload → sees toast, reloads, comes back clean.
- Disconnect network, try to send a message → optimistic message appears then disappears with toast.
- Send 2001-char message → client blocks; server also rejects with typed error.
- Spam AI chat past 20/min → 429 surfaces as a user-visible toast (not console-only).
- Activity feed with >200 entries → trimmed on server, renders without jank.
- PDF export on an empty pipeline → doesn't crash; shows a specific error or degrades.
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes.

## Depends on
Stage 1 (auth) — hardening patterns reference session.fixedUserId for attribution in activity/messages.

## DO NOT
- Do NOT introduce a global state library — keep current dual-write pattern.
- Do NOT add Sentry/analytics.
- Do NOT use external toast libraries.
- Do NOT prescribe exact animation curves or toast positioning — DEV decides.
stages.demoPath: 1. Set SCHEMA_VERSION to 'v2' in src/lib/version.ts, deploy. 2. As a logged-in user with existing localStorage, visit / → toast 'Dashboard updated — refreshing…' appears for ~2s, page reloads, state rehydrates from Mongo. 3. DevTools → throttle to offline, send a chat message → message shows pending then disappears with 'Couldn't send' toast. 4. Spam 25 AI chat messages in 10 seconds → 21st shows rate-limit toast. 5. Add a comment with 1200 chars → client blocks at 1000; server also rejects. 6. Delete all pipelines from Mongo, trigger PDF export → specific empty-state message, no crash.

stages.title: Locked pipelines — enforcement + visual state
stages.spec:
## Why this will be good
Lock is useless if it's only a cosmetic badge. This stage makes 'locked' an actual state: visually unambiguous (you can tell at a glance across the whole dashboard which pipelines are frozen) AND functionally airtight (zero edit paths escape the check). Benchmark: Notion's page-lock, Figma's component-lock, Linear's archived state. Production-ready: locked affordance works on locked dimmed background without losing contrast, lock toggle is discoverable but not in the way, unlock requires the same one-click symmetry, all edit paths (client + server) enforce.

## Goal
Add pipeline-level lock. When locked, every edit interaction is blocked at both the UI layer AND the API layer. Visual state is prominent without being ugly.

## Scope — files to touch
- MODIFY: src/lib/PipelineState.ts — add `lockedPipelines: [String]` to schema (pipeline ids that are locked)
- MODIFY: src/lib/validate.ts — add 'lockedPipelines' to validatePatchKeys whitelist
- MODIFY: src/components/Dashboard.tsx — lockedPipelines state, isLocked(pipelineId) helper, pass to children
- MODIFY: src/components/Stage.tsx — all edit handlers early-return if parent pipeline is locked; visual locked state (dim + lock icon)
- MODIFY: src/components/KanbanView.tsx — disable drag on stages of locked pipelines
- MODIFY: src/components/Dashboard.tsx (header/pipeline header) — lock toggle (padlock button), LOCKED pill badge
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/comments/route.ts — reject with 423 if target pipeline is locked
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/activity/route.ts — reject writes for locked pipelines EXCEPT the lock/unlock action itself
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/route.ts (PATCH) — reject mutations to claims/stageStatusOverrides/subtasks on locked pipelines; allow only lockedPipelines field itself
- NEW or REUSE: small padlock icon (lucide-react if installed, else inline SVG)

## Concrete behavior
- **Edit paths to block when locked:** subtask add/edit/check, comment add, status change, claim/unclaim, reactions, stage drag in kanban, pipeline name/description edit. Messages (chat) are global — NOT blocked.
- **Visual treatment (design intent, DEV executes):** card content feels clearly frozen but stays legible. Suggestions: reduced opacity on interactive elements, padlock icon on pipeline header AND on each stage card header, a LOCKED pill on the pipeline header, pointer-events-none on interactive children, cursor: not-allowed on hover of the card area. Must work on all 4 themes. Must retain accessibility contrast.
- **Lock toggle:** a padlock icon button on each pipeline header. Click to toggle. Available to any authenticated user (MVP). Optimistic + server sync via patchState({ lockedPipelines: [...] }).
- **Server enforcement:** every mutation endpoint checks `if (lockedPipelines.includes(targetPipelineId)) return 423`. The ONLY PATCH allowed for a locked pipeline is changing lockedPipelines itself.
- **Error UX:** if a race causes a client edit attempt to hit 423, show toast 'This pipeline is locked' and refresh local state.
- **Activity log:** log lock/unlock as an activity entry ("Anna locked Pipeline Foo").

## States that must be covered
- Unlocked (default) — no lock affordance visible beyond the toggle itself in a quiet state.
- Locked — pipeline header shows LOCKED pill + active padlock, every stage card feels dimmed/frozen, no interactive elements are usable, padlock button remains active (to unlock).
- Race condition — user on another tab locks the pipeline, current tab tries to edit, server rejects, client reconciles.
- Empty pipeline locked — still visibly locked.
- Mobile — locked affordance survives the small-screen layout (still tappable unlock).

## Acceptance criteria
- Lock a pipeline → LOCKED pill and padlocks appear across ALL its stages.
- Try to check a subtask on a locked pipeline stage → no-op, cursor not-allowed.
- Try to post a comment via API (curl) to a locked pipeline stage → 423 with typed error.
- Unlock → everything returns to normal instantly (no reload).
- Lock state persists across reload (comes from Mongo).
- Activity feed shows 'X locked/unlocked Pipeline Y' entries.
- All UI uses t.* tokens.
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes.

## Depends on
Stages 1 (session.fixedUserId for activity attribution) and 2 (toast primitive for 423 race-condition errors).

## DO NOT
- Do NOT add per-stage locking (pipeline-level only per planner answers).
- Do NOT gate the lock toggle behind a role check for MVP.
- Do NOT prescribe exact opacity values or icon size — DEV picks.
- Do NOT block chat messages (they're cross-pipeline).
stages.demoPath: 1. Sign in, open any pipeline, click the padlock icon on its header → LOCKED pill + padlocks appear on every stage of that pipeline, content dims, activity feed logs 'X locked Pipeline Y'. 2. Try clicking a subtask checkbox → nothing happens, cursor not-allowed. 3. Try to add a comment via curl POST /api/pipeline-state/comments → 423 typed error. 4. In a second browser tab as another user, click unlock → state updates in both tabs (via reload or next fetch). 5. Lock a pipeline on mobile → visual treatment still clear at 390px.

stages.title: Responsive — list / kanban / overview on mobile & tablet
stages.spec:
## Why this will be good
Mobile is currently broken-feeling — panels overflow, kanban is unusable with a thumb, drawers don't exist. This stage makes the dashboard a tool you actually want to open on your phone during a standup. Benchmark: Linear mobile, Height mobile, Notion mobile (bottom sheets done right). Production-ready: thumb-reachable primary actions, 44×44px tap targets, bottom-sheet drawers that feel native (slide-up with inertia, backdrop dismiss, swipe-down dismiss), kanban that either scrolls horizontally with momentum OR collapses to a stacked view (DEV decides after trying both).

## Goal
Every view (list, kanban, overview) is fully usable at 390px and 768px. Chat, ActivityFeed, and expanded stage detail (subtasks/comments/mockup) become bottom-sheet drawers on mobile. No overflow, no broken grids, no tiny tap targets.

## Scope — files to touch
- MODIFY: src/components/Dashboard.tsx — mobile header layout, FAB or header buttons for Chat/Activity access, responsive grid switching
- MODIFY: src/components/ChatPanel.tsx — becomes bottom-sheet drawer on mobile; remains side-panel on desktop
- MODIFY: src/components/ActivityFeed.tsx — becomes bottom-sheet drawer on mobile
- MODIFY: src/components/KanbanView.tsx — decide horizontal-scroll vs stacked on mobile, implement, tap targets ≥44px
- MODIFY: src/components/Stage.tsx — expanded detail (subtasks/comments/mockup) renders as bottom-sheet on mobile
- MODIFY: src/components/OverviewPanel.tsx — stat cards wrap/stack, leaderboard scrolls gracefully
- NEW: src/components/ui/BottomSheet.tsx — reusable drawer primitive (slide-up, backdrop, swipe-down dismiss, focus trap, Escape dismiss)
- MODIFY: src/app/login/page.tsx — verify 390px layout (stage 1 already handles this; revisit if needed)
- FIX (carry-over from stage 3): src/app/api/pipeline-state/activity/route.ts — the POST handler currently has no lock check; add a 2-line guard: read lockedPipelines from the DB doc and return 423 if the event’s pipelineId is in the list AND the event action is not 'lock'/'unlock'. This is the only unimplemented server-side lock guard from stage 3.

## Concrete behavior
- **Breakpoint:** md=768px Tailwind default. Use md: prefixes.
- **BottomSheet primitive:** rendered to document.body via portal, backdrop rgba(0,0,0,0.5), slide-up animation (DEV picks easing), full-screen or 90vh (DEV picks based on content), has a visible drag-handle at the top, dismissible via swipe-down / backdrop click / X button / Escape key. Focus-trapped while open.
- **Chat & Activity on mobile:** accessed via header icons (not buried in a menu). Each opens its own BottomSheet. Only one open at a time.
- **Kanban on mobile:** DEV evaluates two options: (a) horizontal scroll with `scroll-snap`, each column min-w-[80vw], (b) stacked single-column with a status-picker tab bar at top. Pick whichever preserves drag UX better. Document choice in commit message.
- **List view:** cards full-width stack on mobile, 2-col on tablet, existing grid on desktop.
- **Stage card inline layout on mobile:** each stage card in list view has a dense row of inline elements (status pill, points badge, reaction row, claim avatars, subtask count, comment count). On mobile these must not overflow. Acceptable approaches: hide secondary counts behind a '+' toggle, wrap the row with flex-wrap, or stack the meta row below the title. DEV picks whichever fits the card best without truncating critical info (status + points always visible).
- **Pipeline header action bar on mobile:** the per-pipeline header contains multiple action buttons (priority cycle, lock toggle, add stage, pipeline name edit). On mobile these must not overflow the header. Acceptable approach: show only lock toggle + priority pill inline; collapse add-stage and any secondary actions into a compact ⋮ menu button, or wrap to a second line. DEV decides. The lock toggle must always remain immediately tappable (not buried in a menu).
- **Overview:** stat cards wrap to 2-col then 1-col; leaderboard becomes vertically scrollable with sticky header.
- **Expanded stage on mobile:** tapping a stage opens the subtasks/comments/mockup detail as a BottomSheet, not inline-expanded (inline would blow up the page).
- **Tap targets:** every button, checkbox, claim toggle ≥44×44px on mobile. Use padding not margin so the hit area is real.
- **Orientation:** no horizontal overflow at 390px. Test at 320px too (iPhone SE old).

## States that must be covered
- 390px, 768px, 1280px — all three must visibly work.
- BottomSheet empty (empty chat, empty activity) — warm empty state.
- BottomSheet with long scroll content — scrolls independently of backdrop.
- Keyboard up on mobile (typing in chat) — input stays visible above keyboard.
- Locked pipeline on mobile (from stage 3) — locked state still clear.
- Auth denied screen (from stage 1) — reads well on mobile.

## Acceptance criteria
- Resize to 390px: no horizontal scroll anywhere, every interactive target ≥44px, Chat + Activity reachable in one tap.
- Tap Chat icon → BottomSheet slides up, swipe down → dismisses.
- Kanban at 390px: either smoothly horizontal-scrolls column-by-column OR cleanly stacks; dragging a stage works.
- Tap a stage at 390px → BottomSheet with subtasks/comments/mockup.
- Leaderboard on overview at 390px: not clipped, not overflowing.
- POST /api/pipeline-state/activity for a locked pipeline with a non-lock action → returns 423 (carry-over from stage 3).
- All UI uses t.* tokens.
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes.

## Depends on
Stages 1 (login must be mobile-clean already), 2 (toast primitive behaves on mobile too), 3 (locked state must survive mobile treatment).

## DO NOT
- Do NOT install a mobile UI library (react-modal-sheet, vaul, etc.) unless one is already installed — write the BottomSheet ourselves.
- Do NOT prescribe animation curves, drawer heights, icon sizes — DEV decides.
- Do NOT break the desktop layout to fix mobile (responsive additions only, not a redesign).
stages.demoPath: 1. Open /  at 390px viewport (Chrome devtools iPhone 12 Pro). 2. Verify no horizontal scroll. 3. Tap header Chat icon → BottomSheet slides up from bottom → swipe down to dismiss. 4. Tap header Activity icon → BottomSheet opens. 5. Switch to Kanban view → columns either horizontally scroll with snap OR stack with a status tab bar; drag a stage between statuses. 6. Tap any stage → BottomSheet with subtasks + comments + mockup tab. 7. Resize to 768px → layout transitions to tablet (side-panels may return or drawers may remain). 8. Rotate to landscape → no broken layout.

stages.title: Performance — lazy panels, code-split mockups, bundle trim
stages.spec:
## Why this will be good
First paint is what determines whether the dashboard feels 'fast' or 'heavy'. Today ChatPanel, ActivityFeed, KanbanView, OverviewPanel, and ~dozen mockup components all load on first paint even though most users start in List view. This stage splits them out so initial JS shrinks significantly and panels load on demand with honest skeletons — not layout-jarring pop-ins. Benchmark: Vercel dashboard's lazy tabs, Linear's panel lazy-load. Production-ready: skeletons match the real panel's layout (so there's no content-shift when it loads), each lazy chunk is independent (ChatPanel doesn't drag in ActivityFeed), mockup imports are dynamic per-stage so you don't pay for mockups you don't open.

## Goal
Shrink initial bundle by lazy-loading ChatPanel, ActivityFeed, KanbanView, OverviewPanel, and per-stage mockup components. Replace loading gaps with proper skeletons matching final layout.

## Scope — files to touch
- MODIFY: src/components/Dashboard.tsx — replace static imports of ChatPanel/ActivityFeed/KanbanView/OverviewPanel with next/dynamic. Use ssr:false for ChatPanel (uses localStorage + browser APIs) and KanbanView (drag). ActivityFeed and OverviewPanel may allow ssr:true if they have no browser-only APIs on first render.
- MODIFY: src/components/Stage.tsx — replace the static `import mockups from "@/components/mockups/mockupsMap"` with per-stage dynamic loading. See mockupsMap restructure below.
- RESTRUCTURE: src/components/mockups/mockupsMap.tsx — IMPORTANT: the current file exports a plain Record<string, (t: T) => React.ReactNode> with all mockup functions inlined. This cannot be code-split because it is not a component map. To enable per-stage splitting, DEV must convert the map to export lazy React components instead of render functions. Recommended approach: (a) split each mockup (or each pipeline group) into its own file exporting a default React component that accepts { t: T } as props, (b) replace mockupsMap.tsx with a map of next/dynamic(() => import('./mockups/StageName'), { ssr: false, loading: () => <MockupSkeleton /> }) entries. Stage.tsx then renders the lazy component instead of calling the function. An alternative is to keep one file per pipeline section (e.g. mockups-research.tsx, mockups-dev.tsx) and load only the relevant section when a stage in that pipeline is expanded — this gives fewer chunks with meaningful grouping. DEV picks the approach that ships cleanest TypeScript; document choice in commit message.
- NEW: src/components/ui/Skeleton.tsx — themed skeleton primitive (pulse, t.bgCard base, t.border border)
- NEW: small skeletons for ChatPanel, ActivityFeed, KanbanView, OverviewPanel, Mockup — each matches the final panel's shape so no content-jump
- MODIFY: next.config — verify no explicit import that defeats splitting

## Concrete behavior
- **Lazy panels:** use next/dynamic (NOT React.lazy — next/dynamic is the App Router canonical pattern and handles SSR correctly). Each of the 4 big panels becomes its own chunk. First paint only includes Dashboard shell + List view. Switching tabs triggers the lazy load + skeleton → real content.
- **Dynamic mockups:** mockup component for a given stage is loaded only when that stage is expanded. The current architecture (inline render functions in one file) cannot be split as-is — it requires the mockupsMap restructure described above. No sync import of all mockup code.
- **Skeleton requirements:** same outer dimensions as the real panel. Internal shapes (avatar circles, text bars, cards) roughly match so the layout doesn't jump. Uses a subtle pulse animation. No spinner-only placeholders. Skeletons must also work inside BottomSheet (mobile, from stage 4) — sheet animation starts immediately; skeleton fills the open sheet while the chunk loads.
- **SSR:** ssr:false for ChatPanel (localStorage + browser APIs) and KanbanView (drag). Other panels may allow ssr:true if safe.
- **Chunk independence:** verify (post-build) that ChatPanel is NOT co-bundled with ActivityFeed. If both import the same helper and Webpack inlines the helper, that's fine — the point is the panel code itself ships separately.
- **No visible regression:** time-to-open-chat must not feel slower than before (panel is small; lazy-load should complete under 200ms on decent network).

## States that must be covered
- First paint: only List view + shell render. No lazy panel code in initial chunk.
- Open Chat for first time: skeleton for ~tens of ms, then real content.
- Open Chat again: cached, no skeleton.
- Slow 3G: skeleton visible for longer (this is the point — at least something useful renders).
- Mobile (from stage 4): BottomSheet opens → skeleton → content. Sheet animation starts immediately; content fills in as it loads.
- Offline: lazy chunk fails to load → toast (from stage 2 useToasts) + fallback 'Couldn't load. Retry.' button.

## Acceptance criteria
- `next build` output shows ChatPanel, ActivityFeed, KanbanView, OverviewPanel as separate chunks, NOT in main.
- Open DevTools Network → throttle Fast 3G → first paint is noticeably faster than before; panel open shows skeleton then content.
- Mockup for an unopened stage is not in the initial bundle (verify via next build chunk list).
- No layout shift when skeleton → real content.
- All UI uses t.* tokens.
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes.

## Depends on
Stages 1 (auth must not be lazy-loaded — it's before Dashboard), 2 (useToasts / ToastContainer available for chunk-load error handling), 4 (BottomSheets on mobile must show skeletons while lazy chunk loads).

## DO NOT
- Do NOT use React.lazy — use next/dynamic only. React.lazy has SSR limitations in App Router that next/dynamic handles correctly.
- Do NOT add a bundle analyzer dep if one isn't already installed — visual comparison of `next build` output is enough.
- Do NOT lazy-load trivially small components (Stage.tsx itself stays sync; it's the container).
- Do NOT introduce route-level code-splitting beyond what Next.js already does.
- Do NOT prescribe exact skeleton shapes/pulse curves — DEV picks.
stages.demoPath: 1. Run `npm run build`, inspect .next chunks: chat-panel, activity-feed, kanban-view, overview-panel each appear as separate chunks, NOT rolled into main. 2. Deploy, open /, DevTools Network throttle Fast 3G. 3. First paint = List view shell only (no chat/activity/kanban/overview JS in initial). 4. Click Chat tab → skeleton (matching chat layout) appears, then real content fills in — no layout jump. 5. Click Activity tab → separate skeleton, separate chunk loads. 6. Expand a stage → mockup skeleton → mockup component loads. 7. Expand a different stage → its mockup loads separately. 8. Repeat panel open → cached, no skeleton.

stages.title: Email notifications — claim + activation via Gmail SMTP
stages.spec:
## Why this will be good
Right now a claimed stage is silent — the claimer and the rest of the team have no reason to check back. Email notifications close that loop in the lowest-friction way possible: Gmail SMTP is already configured in .env.shared, no new accounts or API keys needed. Benchmark: Linear's issue notifications, Stripe's event emails (clean, branded, skimmable, one-clear-action). Production-ready: emails look like they come from the product (not a raw nodemailer template), don't fire twice for the same event, don't spam in a burst, respect an unsubscribe preference, and never block the PATCH request that triggered them.

## Goal
Send an email to stage claimers when (a) a stage is claimed, (b) a stage goes active. Clean themed HTML template. Fire-and-forget from the PATCH handler. Per-user unsubscribe. Rate-limited to avoid spam.

## What already shipped in earlier stages (DO NOT re-add)
- `nodemailer` and `@types/nodemailer` are already in `dependencies` in package.json (added in stage 1). Do NOT add them again.
- `AuthUser.ts` already has `emailNotifications: { type: Boolean, default: true }` field. Do NOT re-add it.
- `src/lib/auth.ts` already exports `USER_PRIMARY_EMAIL: Record<string, string>` (fixedUserId → primary email) and `getEmailForUser(fixedUserId)`. Import these — do NOT re-declare them.

## Scope — files to touch
- NEW: src/lib/email.ts — nodemailer transport setup using SMTP_* env vars; `sendStageEmail({ to, subject, html })` helper
- NEW: src/lib/emailTemplates.ts — HTML template for stage-claimed and stage-active events (brand-coloured, dark-theme, inline-styled — email clients don't grok CSS vars)
- NEW: src/app/api/unsubscribe/route.ts — GET with signed token → sets user's emailNotifications=false in AuthUser
- MODIFY: src/app/api/pipeline-state/route.ts (PATCH handler) — detect claim additions and status→active transitions, fire notification(s) without awaiting, log errors silently
- NEW: src/lib/notifyRateLimit.ts — per-user cap (e.g. max 1 email per stage event per 5 minutes)
- NEW: src/components/NotificationPrefs.tsx — small toggle in the user menu/profile area for 'Email notifications' (writes to AuthUser via PATCH /api/auth/prefs)
- NEW: src/app/api/auth/prefs/route.ts — PATCH handler: reads session, updates AuthUser.emailNotifications in MongoDB
- FIX (carry-over from stage 5): src/components/mockups/mockupsMap.tsx — the `makeSkeleton()` loading placeholder uses a hardcoded `background: "rgba(255,255,255,0.04)"`. Change this to `background: "rgba(128,128,128,0.06)"` (2-character change — neutral gray reads acceptably across all 4 themes without a t.* token, since next/dynamic loading() cannot receive props).

## Concrete behavior
- **SMTP config** (already in /Users/zoop/.env.shared): SMTP_HOST=smtp.gmail.com, SMTP_USER=binayahmedia@gmail.com, SMTP_PASS (app password), SMTP_PORT=465 (secure:true). DEV must ensure these are set in Vercel for the ultimate project — use the Vercel API per CLAUDE.md rules. If any SMTP_* env var is missing, sendStageEmail throws a typed error that's caught silently.
- **Triggers:**
  - Claim added: when PATCH sets `claims[stageKey]` to include a new fixedUserId. Notify the newly-added claimer(s) (not everyone).
  - Status → active: when PATCH sets `stageStatusOverrides[stageKey]` to 'active'. Notify every claimer currently on that stage.
- **Recipient lookup:** call `getEmailForUser(fixedUserId)` from `src/lib/auth.ts` (already exported). Skip users whose AuthUser.emailNotifications=false (look up from MongoDB by email).
- **Template:** HTML with hardcoded inline hex colours that match the war-room palette (email clients strip <style> and CSS vars). Header bar in brand purple (#bf5af2), dark background (#08050f), body with stage name + pipeline name + actor name + timestamp (human format), primary CTA button linking to https://dashboard-gamification.vercel.app/?pipeline=X&stage=Y (deep link — DEV implements a simple query-param focus on dashboard load), footer with 'Unsubscribe' link using a signed token per-user.
- **Deep link:** Dashboard reads `?pipeline=` and `?stage=` on mount and scrolls/focuses the target stage.
- **Unsubscribe:** GET /api/unsubscribe?t=<signed-token>. Verify HMAC (secret from NEXTAUTH_SECRET or a new UNSUBSCRIBE_SECRET). Set emailNotifications=false on the matching AuthUser. Show a simple 'You’re unsubscribed — resubscribe in your profile' page.
- **Rate limiting:** max 1 email per (userId, stageKey, eventType) per 5 minutes. Stored in an in-memory Map (serverless caveat: best-effort per lambda instance; acceptable for MVP).
- **Fire-and-forget:** `sendStageEmail({...}).catch(err => console.error('email failed', err))` — never await in the PATCH handler. The PATCH returns whatever it would have returned; the email is separate.
- **User preferences UI:** a small 'Email me when a stage I claimed goes live' toggle in the user menu. PATCH /api/auth/prefs with `{ emailNotifications: boolean }`. Reads session.fixedUserId to find the AuthUser record by fixedUserId (or email, whichever is simpler given the schema).
- **Subject format:** `[Binayah Dashboard] {stage} is {now live|claimed by {actor}}`.
- **From:** `Binayah Dashboard <binayahmedia@gmail.com>`.

## States that must be covered
- Happy path: claim → claimer receives email within a few seconds.
- Missing SMTP config: silent skip, logs warning — no crash.
- User has emailNotifications=false: no email sent to them.
- Rate-limited (same event fired twice in <5 min): second send is suppressed.
- Invalid unsubscribe token: error page with specific message.
- Deep link to non-existent stage: dashboard loads normally, no scroll (no crash).
- Mobile email rendering: template must look decent in Gmail mobile, Apple Mail, Outlook web (inline styles, tables-for-layout if needed).

## Acceptance criteria
- Claim a stage as Anna → Anna receives email at anna@prosper-fi.com within ~10s.
- Click the CTA in the email → opens dashboard focused on the claimed stage.
- Click unsubscribe in the email → lands on success page, AuthUser.emailNotifications=false.
- After unsubscribe, claim another stage → no email.
- Toggle emailNotifications back on in UI → emails resume.
- Set stage to active → every current claimer gets an 'is now live' email.
- Spam-click claim/unclaim → only one email fires per 5-min window per (user, stage, event).
- Remove SMTP_PASS from Vercel env → claim still works, email is silently skipped, warning in server logs.
- All UI uses t.* tokens; email template uses inline hex colours matching brand (no t.* in email HTML).
- mockupsMap.tsx loading placeholder no longer uses white-tinted rgba — uses neutral rgba(128,128,128,0.06).
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes.

## Depends on
Stages 1 (ADMIN_EMAIL_MAP + AuthUser model + session + USER_PRIMARY_EMAIL already in auth.ts), 2 (typed API errors + toast for unsubscribe page failures), 3 (locked pipelines — a claim on a locked pipeline shouldn't happen, but if it somehow does, the notification path mustn't crash), 4 (prefs toggle must work on mobile), 5 (NotificationPrefs is small enough to not need lazy-loading, skip).

## DO NOT
- Do NOT use Twilio or WhatsApp — planner confirmed email-only via existing Gmail SMTP.
- Do NOT await the email send in the PATCH handler — fire-and-forget.
- Do NOT put the SMTP password anywhere but .env.shared + Vercel env.
- Do NOT re-add nodemailer to package.json or emailNotifications to AuthUser.ts or USER_PRIMARY_EMAIL/getEmailForUser to auth.ts — all three already exist from stage 1.
- Do NOT prescribe exact email colours/spacing — DEV picks, but it must look like the product, not a plaintext fallback.
stages.demoPath: 1. Ensure SMTP_* vars are set in Vercel for the ultimate project (push via Vercel API if missing). 2. Sign in as Anna (anna@prosper-fi.com). 3. Claim any unclaimed stage → within ~10s, anna@prosper-fi.com inbox has '[Binayah Dashboard] {stage} is claimed by Anna' email. 4. Click CTA → browser opens dashboard focused on that stage. 5. Click 'Unsubscribe' in email footer → success page renders. 6. In a second browser as another user, change a stage Anna claimed to 'active' → Anna gets NO email (unsubscribed), but any other claimer on that stage does. 7. In Anna's user menu, toggle 'Email notifications' back on → next claim event sends an email. 8. Rapidly toggle claim on the same stage 5 times in 30 seconds → only 1 email fires.

Right now a claimed stage is silent — the claimer and the rest of the team have no reason to check back. Email notifications close that loop in the lowest-friction way possible: Gmail SMTP is already configured in .env.shared, no new accounts or API keys needed. Benchmark: Linear's issue notifications, Stripe's event emails (clean, branded, skimmable, one-clear-action). Production-ready: emails look like they come from the product (not a raw nodemailer template), don't fire twice for the same event, don't spam in a burst, respect an unsubscribe preference, and never block the PATCH request that triggered them.

Header bar in brand purple (#bf5af2), dark background (#08050f), body with stage name + pipeline name + actor name + timestamp (human format), primary CTA button linking to https://dashboard-gamification.vercel.app/?pipeline=X&stage=Y (deep link — DEV implements a simple query-param focus on dashboard load), footer with 'Unsubscribe' link using a signed token per-user.

devReport.summary: Email notifications (stage 6): SMTP transport, claim+active templates, per-user rate limit, HMAC unsubscribe, /api/auth/prefs, NotificationPrefs toggle, deep-link ?pipeline=&stage= in Dashboard; mockupsMap skeleton rgba carry-over fix

designBrief.direction: no aesthetic direction needed for this scope
designBrief.hero: Email templates use hardcoded war-room hex palette (#08050f bg, #bf5af2 header bar, #f0ecff text, #30d158 active accent) — email clients strip CSS vars
designBrief.cards: Table-based layout with a branded header bar for email client compatibility
designBrief.interactions: Toggle pill for NotificationPrefs in user stats popup (t.accent when enabled, t.border when disabled)
designBrief.copy: Command-center voice: // stage claimed, // stage is live, // notifications disabled
designBrief.benchmark: Linear issue notifications + Stripe event emails — clean, branded, one clear action

selfGrade.evidence: email.ts: nodemailer createTransport using SMTP_HOST/PORT/USER/PASS/SECURE; sendStageEmail helper

selfGrade.action: Create src/lib/emailTemplates.ts with claim + active templates
selfGrade.evidence: emailTemplates.ts: claimEmailTemplate + activeEmailTemplate with inline hex, CTA, unsubscribe link
notifyRateLimit.ts: checkNotifyRateLimit(fixedUserId, stageKey, eventType) → boolean, 5-min window
sendNotifications.ts: checks emailNotifications pref in MongoDB, rate limit, builds unsubscribe token, fires sendStageEmail.catch()

selfGrade.action: Wire PATCH route for claim + active events
selfGrade.evidence: route.ts: pre-patch state capture, diff detection, void sendNotifications() after findOneAndUpdate
unsubscribe/route.ts: buildUnsubscribeToken (HMAC-sha256), timingSafeEqual verify, HTML confirmation page
prefs/route.ts: session-guarded, updates AuthUser.emailNotifications, returns { emailNotifications }
NotificationPrefs.tsx: toggle pill using t.accent/t.border, fetches GET /api/auth/prefs on mount, PATCHes on toggle

selfGrade.action: Wire NotificationPrefs into Dashboard user stats popup
selfGrade.evidence: Dashboard.tsx: import NotificationPrefs, renders inside isMe block after change avatar button
Dashboard.tsx: useEffect reads window.location.search, expands pipeline, sets expS, scrolls to #stage-{name}

selfGrade.action: Push SMTP env vars to Vercel
selfGrade.evidence: Confirmed via Vercel API: SMTP_USER, SMTP_PASS, SMTP_HOST, SMTP_PORT, SMTP_SECURE already present on production+preview
.env.local: SMTP_USER, SMTP_PASS, SMTP_HOST, SMTP_PORT, SMTP_SECURE added from .env.shared

findings: minor: toggle knob color token fixed
T5/T6 fixed — unsubscribe accessible without auth

summary: trimmed stage 6 spec — nodemailer/emailNotifications/USER_PRIMARY_EMAIL already shipped in stage 1; added loading-placeholder rgba fix from stage 5 carry-over; added /api/auth/prefs route to scope

stageHistory.summary: Auth foundation — next-auth, login UI, Prajeesh, middleware gate. 2 minor copy bugs carried forward.
Cache recovery guard, Toast system, typed errors, rollbacks, bounds, offline indicator. All fixes verified.

Lock bypass fix: removed _pipelineId early-return null, custom DB stages merged into stageToP. 13+ client guards, visual lock state, KanbanView drag disabled, 423 on PATCH+comments. Minor: activity route has no lock check (logging-only, not blocking).
Locked pipelines — full enforcement (13 guards), visual state, API 423, security bypass fix, MongoDB persistence.
Responsive layout — BottomSheet, mobile stage cards, Kanban snap-scroll, pipeline header collapse, activity lock guard. 0 bugs.
Lazy panels (next/dynamic), code-split mockups (8 chunks), skeletons, ErrorBoundary. Build: 32 chunks, 0 TS errors.

plannerAnswers:
1. Default — map by email to fixed ID using ADMIN_EMAILS whitelist
2. Whitelist only — but multiple emails can map to same user (e.g. anna@prosper-fi.com AND anna@gmail.com both map to anna)
3. Default — https://dashboard-gamification.vercel.app
4. WhatsApp notifications instead of email — send to claimers on the stage when claimed or goes active
5. Agent decides best UX — should be perfect, not just functional
6. Default — 2-second toast then auto-clear and reload
7. Default — pipeline-level lock only
Extra: everything must be production-ready — messaging, every function, all edge cases, not just the 6 listed areas
NOTIFICATIONS FINAL: Use email (Gmail SMTP via nodemailer). Already configured in .env.shared. Send to all stage claimers when stage claimed or goes active.
Prajeesh: add as 6th team member. pm@binayah.com → prajeesh. Role: PM. Color: pick something distinct.
mamr@binayah.com → ahsan (existing user)

contextGaps.missing: next-auth is not installed yet; auth will be added in stage 1 via next-auth v4 (App Router compatible). Keep this in PROJECT_CONTEXT once shipped.

Add prajeesh (pm@binayah.com, role 'The PM') to USERS_DEFAULT as the 6th user. ADMIN_EMAIL_MAP = { anna@prosper-fi.com→anna, dev@prosper-fi.com→anna, anna@gmail.com→anna, aakarshit@prosper-fi.com→aakarshit, uk@prosper-fi.com→usama, mamr@binayah.com→ahsan, pm@binayah.com→prajeesh }.
NEXTAUTH_URL for binayah-ultimate Vercel project must be https://dashboard-gamification.vercel.app — distinct from the shared .env.shared value used by binayah-properties.
No WhatsApp/Twilio credentials exist in .env.shared. Notifications use Gmail SMTP (binayahmedia@gmail.com) via nodemailer; decision finalised per plannerAnswers.
DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §Typography documents Geist Sans/Mono but layout.tsx uses DM Sans/DM Mono (--font-dm-sans, --font-dm-mono). Update DESIGN_SYSTEM.md at ship time.

Portal overlay unmount pattern: position:fixed inset:0 portals (BottomSheet, modals) must clear visible state after close animation via setTimeout/transitionend, otherwise the invisible overlay permanently intercepts pointer events.

next/dynamic loading() prop does NOT receive component props — cannot pass theme token `t` to loading placeholders. Workaround: use a static placeholder in loading() and rely on Suspense fallback (with t) for themed skeletons, or inject keyframes separately.

designBrief.direction: Command-center front door — not a login form. A dense, dark, purpose-built threshold that looks like a fragment of the product itself, defaulting to War Room (neon purple #bf5af2 on #08050f) with a Geist-Mono HUD texture and confident lowercase copy.
designBrief.directionReasoning: The brief explicitly names this the first surface every team member sees, and the product's soul is the War Room theme. A generic centered card would erase everything that makes Binayah Ultimate feel like a command center before the user has even signed in. Linear's sign-in reduces the form to its essence on a black field; Superhuman's invite screen treats the screen as product chrome, not marketing. We want both: a single lockup, a single line of HUD copy, a primary Google action, and an email path that feels like opening a side hatch — not a second form bolted on.

benchmark.product: Linear sign-in (desktop) crossed with Superhuman's invite screen — both on a pure black field with ONE focal lockup, generous negative space, and zero marketing ornament. Secondary reference: Vercel dashboard login's quiet hierarchy (primary CTA dominates, everything else is a hairline).
benchmark.whatToMatch: The feeling that you've arrived at a tool, not a landing page. A single column, tight max-width (~360px), brand mark top, one line of mono tagline under it, primary CTA full-width, secondary paths as hairline-divided text links — not second buttons. The page breathes; nothing is shouting except the accent glow.

surfaces.name: Background field — the ambient signal
decisions: Full viewport t.bg (#08050f warroom dark). Not a card floated on a gradient — the page IS the card.

A single ambient light source: a very soft radial halo of t.accent at ~8% alpha, ~600px radius, anchored behind the lockup. This is the only 'decoration' on the screen. It reads as a glow bleeding through the hull, not a background image.
Optional second layer: a 1px grid of t.border at ~40% alpha, subtractive — visible only where the halo falls off. If it adds noise, cut it. Halo alone is enough.
No hero image. No illustration. No marketing.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: signal density is a virtue — but density on a login means the details are intentional, not that the screen is busy. The halo makes the page feel alive (command center breathes); the grid gives the mono copy a HUD surface to sit on. Anything more decorative and it becomes a landing page.

surfaces.name: Brand lockup
decisions: Top-of-column, ~96px down from viewport top on desktop, ~48px on mobile — NOT vertically centered with the form. The form sits below and reads as the subordinate element.
Line 1: the War Room icon glyph (🏴‍☠️) at 28px, followed by 'Binayah Ultimate' in Geist Sans 700, t.text, 20px.

Line 2: the theme tagline in Geist Mono, t.textDim, italic, 12px — '// where strategies are forged'. This is the same t.sub used in-product, rendered identically. The user sees the product's voice before they touch it.
Alignment: left-aligned (not centered). Left-alignment reads as 'system', centered reads as 'marketing'. The whole column is left-aligned.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: copy is the culture. The mono tagline is already the product's signature voice; putting it here before anything else says 'you're not on a marketing page, you're already inside.' Left alignment is the command-center grammar — Linear, Raycast, Superhuman all left-align their auth surfaces.

surfaces.name: Primary action — Continue with Google
decisions: Full-width button, 44px tall (tap-target), background t.accent, text t.bg (the only place on the page where t.bg is on top of t.accent — this is the loudest element).
Inside: a 16px Google 'G' glyph (use the official multicolor SVG — do NOT tint it to the theme), 10px gap, label 'continue with google' in Geist Sans 600, 14px, lowercase.

Resting shadow: 0 0 24px {t.accent}33 — a soft purple glow that reinforces the halo. On hover: glow intensifies to {t.accent}55, button lifts 1px (translateY(-1px)), 150ms ease-out. On press: returns to 0, no glow pulse (press is instant, not celebratory).
Loading state: label becomes 'opening google…' lowercase, spinner (3px border, t.bg on t.accent) replaces the G glyph in the same slot. Button stays full t.accent — does NOT go gray. Disabled=true.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: every surface responds, but motion has meaning. The glow is ambient product personality; the lift on hover is the button acknowledging you. The lowercase 'continue with google' matches the product's irreverent register ('gm legend' energy) — 'Sign in with Google' would read enterprise-neutral.

surfaces.name: Divider + secondary email path
decisions: Below the Google button, ~20px gap, a horizontal hairline: 1px t.border, full column width, with the word 'or' centered on it in Geist Mono 11px, t.textDim, on a t.bg pill (8px horizontal padding) that visually breaks the line. Not 'OR' capital — lowercase mono.
Below the divider: a single line of plaintext link 'sign in with email →' in Geist Mono 13px, t.textSec. The arrow is a real character (→), not an icon. Hover: color shifts to t.accent, 150ms.

Clicking expands the email form INLINE below the link, same column width, 200ms ease-out slide+fade. The link text swaps to 'hide ↑' while expanded. This is a toggle, not a route change — no layout jump.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: hierarchy is earned. Google is the primary path — 5 of 7 whitelisted emails are @prosper-fi or @binayah Google Workspace accounts. Email/password is the fallback for the rare case. Rendering it as a text link, not a second button, makes the hierarchy unambiguous without hiding the path.

surfaces.name: Email form (expanded state)
decisions: Two inputs stacked, 8px gap. Each: full-width, 44px tall, background t.surface, 1px border t.border, 8px radius, 13px Geist Sans, t.text, 12px horizontal padding.
Focus state: border becomes t.accent, box-shadow 0 0 0 3px {t.accent}22 (same halo vocabulary). 150ms.
Placeholder copy (t.textDim): email field = 'you@binayah.com', password field = 'your password'. Lowercase, specific — not 'Email address' / 'Enter password'.

Below the inputs: a row with two elements on opposite ends. Left: a small mono link 'first time? set a password →' (toggles the form to signup mode — same two fields, CTA label changes). Right: an inline 'sign in' button, 36px tall, t.accent background, t.bg text, auto-width, padding 0 16px. On signup mode: the button reads 'create access'.
Enter key in either field submits the form. Tab order: email → password → submit button → 'first time?' link → back to Google button. Autofocus email field on expand.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: gamification must feel earned — and so must access. 'create access' reads as earning a keycard, not filling a form. The t.accent halo on focus echoes the button glow — the screen has ONE accent color and every interactive surface uses it, so the user's eye tracks between them without friction.

surfaces.name: Error / deny states — the designed rejection
decisions: All errors render INLINE, directly below the offending form (or below the Google button for OAuth failures), 12px gap. Never a toast, never a modal, never a red alert box.
Container: no background, no border. Just text. 12px Geist Mono, color t.red, left-aligned. A preceding '//' in t.textDim, then the message in t.red. Reads like a console line, not an error dialog.

Specific messages (exact copy): wrong password = '// access denied — wrong email or password'. Not whitelisted = '// {email} isn't on the access list. ping prajeesh.' The user's typed email is rendered inline in t.text (not t.red) so they can verify the typo. Network/unknown = '// signal lost. try again.' Signup without whitelist = '// {email} isn't on the access list — signups are invite-only.'

Motion: error appears with a 150ms fade + 4px slide-up. If a new error replaces an old one, the old fades out first (100ms), then the new fades in. No jitter, no shake. The command center doesn't panic.
The form does NOT auto-clear on error — the user's email stays so they can see what they typed. Password clears only on explicit credential error, not on whitelist error.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: empty states (and error states) are part of the game. A red alert box on a dark command screen would look like a Windows XP dialog. Rendering errors as console lines ('// access denied') keeps the HUD grammar intact — the product is telling you what happened in its own voice. Naming the email back to the user is table-stakes production polish; framing the denial as 'ping prajeesh' is the irreverent register the product speaks.

surfaces.name: Already-signed-in redirect
decisions: If the user lands on /login already authenticated, do NOT render the login UI at all. Render a single centered mono line 'signed in — redirecting…' in t.textDim, 13px, and fire router.replace('/') immediately in a useEffect.
No flash of the form. The middleware catches most of this, but the /login route itself also guards.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: loading is activity, not waiting. A 50ms flash of the login form before redirect would feel like a bug. The mono 'redirecting…' line is the same visual vocabulary as the error state — one grammar for all machine messages.

surfaces.name: Mobile layout (≤390px)
decisions: Column max-width = viewport - 48px (24px horizontal padding each side). Lockup at 48px from top. Halo still present but smaller (~400px radius).
Google button stays 44px tall, full-width of column. Email expansion stays inline — do NOT route to a new screen.
Typography scale unchanged — the page already has a tight type scale; don't shrink further.

No keyboard-avoidance tricks — on focus, the browser handles scroll. Verify the 'sign in' button stays above the keyboard when password is focused (bottom-sheet-style fixed positioning NOT used here; just normal flow).
surfaces.whyThisWay: The brief explicitly says 'doesn't feel like a shrink-fit desktop form.' Keeping the exact same composition — lockup top, button primary, email inline — at 390px means the mobile user sees the same command center, not a compressed version.

surfaces.name: Footer — the HUD timestamp
decisions: Fixed to bottom-left of viewport, 24px from edges. Single line of Geist Mono, 10px, t.textDim.

Content: 'binayah ultimate · v{SCHEMA_VERSION} · {currentYear}' — reads like a build tag in the corner of a mission-control monitor. No logo, no legal links, no 'Terms of Service' (this is invite-only; there is no terms page to link).
Do NOT fix to bottom-center or bottom-right; left is where a HUD watermark sits.
surfaces.whyThisWay: Principle: signal density is a virtue. One line of HUD text in the corner makes the screen feel inhabited by the product, not a blank login. It also gives QA/devs a visible version tag on the front door — useful for the Stage 2 cache-version work.

designBrief.motion: All timing honors the product's calibrated scale from DESIGN_PRINCIPLES: 150ms for micro (hover lift, focus ring), 300ms for state changes (email form expand/collapse, error enter/exit is 150ms/100ms as a sub-case). Halo is static — no pulsing background. Google button glow intensifies on hover, not on resting. Form expansion: 200ms ease-out slide+fade, height auto (not fixed px — let content define). Login→dashboard transition: on successful auth, fade the whole page out at 200ms to t.bg, then router.push('/') — the dashboard fades in on its own. No explicit celebration animation on login success (celebration is reserved for claim events, per principles — don't cheapen it by firing on sign-in).

copy.voice: Tactical, confident, lowercase. Mono HUD for system messages, sans for user-facing CTAs. Matches the 'gm legend' / '// where strategies are forged' register already established in the product. Never capitalize sentences; never say 'please' or 'welcome'; do say 'access', 'signal', 'ping', 'territory'.
forbidden: Access Denied (capitalized like a Windows error)
Terms of Service / Privacy Policy links
Don't have an account? Sign up (see replacement)

replacements.Wrong credentials error: // access denied — wrong email or password
replacements.Not-whitelisted error: // {email} isn't on the access list. ping prajeesh.
replacements.Signup-not-whitelisted error: // {email} isn't on the access list — signups are invite-only.

designBrief.colorExtensions: Uses existing War Room palette only. No new tokens. The halo/glow effects are derived inline from t.accent with alpha suffixes (t.accent + '22' for borders-on-focus, t.accent + '33' for resting button glow, t.accent + '55' for hover glow, t.accent + '08' or similar for the ambient radial) — this is the documented pattern in DESIGN_SYSTEM.md. All 4 themes must be supported (per stage spec: 'Must support all 4 themes'), but /login defaults to warroom unless a saved theme exists in localStorage. When rendered in lab/engine/nerve, every token swap cleanly because we used t.* throughout — no warroom-specific hex anywhere.
designBrief.layoutSketch:
Desktop (≥768px):

+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                          |
|   [48px top gutter]                                      |
|                                                          |
|   [lockup column — left-aligned, max-w 360px,            |
|    horizontally centered in viewport]                    |
|                                                          |
|   🏴‍☠️  Binayah Ultimate                                  |
|   // where strategies are forged                         |
|                                                          |
|   [64px gap]                                             |
|                                                          |
|   [ G  continue with google         ]  <- full-width     |
|                                                          |
|   ───────────── or ─────────────                         |
|                                                          |
|   sign in with email →                                   |
|                                                          |
|   (expanded state adds below:)                           |
|   [ you@binayah.com                  ]                   |
|   [ your password                    ]                   |
|   first time? set a password →    [ sign in ]            |
|                                                          |
|   // access denied — wrong email or password             |
|                                                          |
|                                                          |
|                                                          |
| binayah ultimate · v2 · 2026                             |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
      \__ ambient radial halo of t.accent at ~8% alpha, centered behind lockup

Mobile (≤390px): identical composition, 24px side gutters, 48px top gutter, halo ~400px. No layout restructure — same elements, same order.
doNot: Do NOT center the lockup vertically on the viewport — it sits in the top third. Vertical-centered forms read as marketing landing pages.
Do NOT put the form inside a bordered card floating on the background. The viewport IS the surface. A card-on-bg is the generic auth pattern we're rejecting.
Do NOT use a red-background error box or toast for errors. Errors render as inline mono console lines in t.red text on t.bg.

Do NOT render Google button icon tinted to the theme accent — it's the official multicolor Google G. This is the one place we break theme grammar, because brand-recognition of the G > aesthetic purity.
Do NOT capitalize CTA copy. Lowercase is load-bearing for the product voice.
Do NOT add 'Forgot password?' — it's invite-only, whitelist-based, tiny team; password reset flow is out of scope and the link would look enterprise.
Do NOT add marketing/landing content: no feature list, no illustration, no testimonials, no 'what is this?' explainer.
Do NOT animate the halo (no pulse, no breathing effect). It's ambient, not alive. Motion is reserved for interaction response.

Do NOT fire a celebration animation (claim-style float-up, points flash) on successful login. Celebration is sacred to claim/point events per DESIGN_PRINCIPLES — firing it here cheapens it everywhere.
Do NOT render the email form behind a route change (/login/email). It's an inline toggle on the same page.
Do NOT use Tailwind gray-* or neutral-* classes for text colors. Every color comes from t.* tokens — including error red (t.red).

Do NOT show a skeleton/spinner on the login page itself while SSR hydrates — the page is small and should render server-side with no loading state. Loading state belongs ONLY on in-flight button presses.
designBrief.signupVsLogin: Same page, inline toggle inside the email form — NOT a separate /signup route, NOT a tab switcher at the top. The Google path is identical for signup and login (Google OAuth doesn't need a separate signup flow — the whitelist check gates both). The email form toggles between 'sign in' and 'create access' modes via a single text link below the inputs ('first time? set a password →' in login mode, '← back to sign in' in signup mode). The visual difference between modes is exactly one word: the submit button label. Inputs stay identical. This keeps the surface a single unified door — no user ever has to ask 'am I on the right page?'
designBrief.transition: Post-login: the page fades to t.bg at 200ms (opacity 1→0 on the lockup column only, background stays), then router.push('/') runs. The dashboard's own mount animation (Onboarding fade-in or Dashboard shell) takes over. No full-page flash, no white screen. If session already exists on /login mount, render ONLY the 'signed in — redirecting…' mono line (no form, no lockup) and redirect immediately — do not let the form flash.

stateCoverageMatrix.resting: Lockup + halo + Google button + divider + 'sign in with email →' link + footer. Email form collapsed.
stateCoverageMatrix.email_expanded: Adds two inputs + submit button + signup toggle link below the 'sign in with email' link (which becomes 'hide ↑').
stateCoverageMatrix.in_flight_google: Google button: icon→spinner, label→'opening google…', disabled. All other interactions disabled. No visible change elsewhere.
stateCoverageMatrix.in_flight_credentials: Submit button: label→'verifying…', disabled. Inputs become readonly (not disabled — keep text visible). Google button disabled but visible.
stateCoverageMatrix.in_flight_signup: Submit button: label→'provisioning…', otherwise same as credentials in-flight.
stateCoverageMatrix.error_wrong_password: Inline mono line below form. Password field clears. Email stays. Email field gets keyboard focus.
stateCoverageMatrix.error_not_whitelisted: Inline mono line naming the email. Nothing clears. Email field stays focused on what they typed.
stateCoverageMatrix.error_network: Inline mono line 'signal lost. try again.'. Nothing clears.
stateCoverageMatrix.already_signed_in: Only the 'signed in — redirecting…' mono line renders. No lockup, no form, no footer. Redirect fires immediately.
stateCoverageMatrix.theme_override: If localStorage has a saved theme other than warroom, render in that theme. Every token swaps cleanly. The Google G glyph stays multicolor in all themes.
